shower.thought: We spend our whole lives learning how to be human, but nobody teaches us what to do once we've figured it out.
Your childhood fears made sense at the time, which means your current anxieties probably will too—eventually.
The only time you see your face is when you're looking at a reflection, so you never actually see what other people see when they look at you.
We call it "falling in love" but we're really just jumping and hoping someone catches us.
You only know you've hit rock bottom when you stop digging, which means the bottom isn't real—it's just a choice.
Confidence is just a decision to stop explaining yourself to people in your head.
The most honest conversations happen at 2 AM because exhaustion defeats whatever we've built up during the day.
You're probably more interesting to other people than you think, and more boring to them than you fear—rarely either extreme.
Every argument about the past is really an argument about what it means for the future.
The people you most want to impress are usually the ones least equipped to understand why you're trying.
You can't actually remember most of your life—you just remember the stories you've told about it.
Happiness might not be the goal; maybe it's just evidence that you're going the right direction.
We assume people who disagree with us are either stupid or evil, which is usually just another way of saying "different."
Your phone knows more about your personality than your closest friend does.
The saddest part of growing up isn't responsibility—it's realizing your parents were just making it up as they went along too.
You spend decades becoming the person you think you should be, then the rest of your life unlearning it.
Most of what we call "bad luck" is just information we didn't have in time.
The things you regret most aren't usually things you did—they're things you didn't try because you were afraid of the outcome.
We romanticize the past because we forget how uncertain it felt while we were living it.
Loneliness is just a mismatch between how you want to connect and how people around you are willing to.

motivational.thought: Your mess is not your destiny. It's just the material you're working with. — Unknown
The smallest act of courage today becomes the biggest part of your story tomorrow. — Unknown
You can't think your way into becoming someone new. You have to act your way there. — Unknown
Comparison is the thief of progress. Your only competition was yesterday. — Unknown
What feels impossible now is just unfamiliar. Do it enough times and it becomes ordinary. — Unknown
The person who says it can't be done shouldn't interrupt the person doing it. — Unknown
Your potential isn't some fixed thing waiting to be discovered. It's something you build with every choice. — Unknown
Most people overestimate what they can do in a year and underestimate what they can do in a decade. — Unknown
You're not behind. You're not late. You're exactly where your decisions have brought you. — Unknown
The only person keeping score is you. Everyone else is too busy with their own game. — Unknown
Discipline is just choosing what you want most over what you want right now. — Unknown
It's not about having time. It's about making time for what matters. — Unknown
Your excuses are costing you more than your effort ever will. — Unknown
The person you'll regret not being is right there, waiting for you to choose her. — Unknown
Comfort is a slow form of suffering. — Unknown
You don't need permission to become who you want to be. You never did. — Unknown
The work you avoid is the work that's trying to teach you something. — Unknown
Real confidence isn't believing you'll win. It's moving forward even if you might lose. — Unknown
Your future is being decided in the moments you think don't matter. — Unknown
The only way out is through. But the way through is shorter than you think. — Unknown

darkjoke.thought:
I told my girlfriend she'd be perfect if she just lost a few pounds.
She did. We haven't spoken in months.
I'm not saying my life is a disaster, but my autobiography is being published in the self-help section.

My therapist asked what my biggest fear is.
I said "Ending up like you—listening to people's problems for money."

I've started journaling to process my emotions.
Turns out my emotions are just "tired" and "also tired but angry."

My dad said he was proud of me for the first time.
Turns out he was talking to someone on the phone behind me.

I told my friend I was struggling with addiction.
He said "To what?"
I said "Honestly, I'm not that committed."

My doctor said I need to cut back on screen time.
I said "Doc, my entire personality is curated by algorithms—I think we're past preventative measures."

I asked my parents why they had me.
They said "It seemed like a good idea at the time."
I said "And now?"
They said "Still seems like a good idea. For you to leave."

My gym membership costs more than my rent.
At least I'm consistent about not using them both.

I love when people say "I'm going through a lot right now."
Like buddy, we're all just slowly deteriorating—some of us just have paperwork about it.

My ex said I never listened to her.
At least that's what I assume happened. I wasn't really paying attention.

I started meditating to find inner peace.
Turns out it was just inner quiet.
The peace never showed up.

My boss said I have a lot of untapped potential.
I said "Yeah, it's going to stay untapped."
He didn't get it. He will eventually.

I told my date I was emotionally unavailable.
She said "Good thing I'm not emotionally available either."
We're perfect together until we're not.

My insurance company denied my claim.
I called to complain and got transferred to someone who also doesn't care.
At least we were honest with each other.

I said to my friend, "I'm thinking about getting sober."
He said "Why?"
I had no answer because he has a valid point.

My LinkedIn profile says I'm "passionate about growth."
That's just a nice way of saying I'm desperate and willing to relocate.

I asked the cashier how her day was going.
She said "You don't actually care."
We stared at each other in mutual recognition.

My therapist asked if I was making progress.
I said "I'm now aware of my problems instead of just being confused."
She said "That's something."
I said "It really isn't."

My friend said he's finally figured out what he wants in life.
I'm happy for him—must be nice having that level of delusion.
